title: From Zero to Hero: Building REST APIs with Node.js and Express
goal: Build REST APIs with Node.js and Express

modules.title: Module 1: Introduction to Node.js and NPM
modules.description: Learn what Node.js is, how to install it, and use NPM to manage packages.
objectives: Understand Node.js and its runtime environment
Install Node.js and NPM on your system
Create and manage a Node.js project using NPM

modules.title: Module 2: Getting Started with Express.js
modules.description: Set up a basic Express server and understand routing fundamentals.
objectives: Install and set up Express.js in a Node.js project
Create a basic server with Express
Define and handle routes using HTTP methods

modules.title: Module 3: Working with Middleware
modules.description: Explore middleware in Express and how it processes requests.
objectives: Understand the concept and role of middleware in Express
Use built-in middleware like express.json() and express.urlencoded()
Create custom middleware functions

modules.title: Module 4: Handling Requests and Responses
modules.description: Learn to process incoming requests and send appropriate responses.
objectives: Extract data from request objects (params, query, body)
Send responses with different status codes
Handle errors and send meaningful error responses

modules.title: Module 5: Connecting to a Database
modules.description: Integrate a database (MongoDB) with your Express API using Mongoose.
objectives: Set up MongoDB and connect it to your Node.js application
Define and interact with data models using Mongoose
Perform CRUD operations with Mongoose

modules.title: Module 6: Building a RESTful API
modules.description: Design and implement a complete RESTful API with Express and MongoDB.
objectives: Understand REST principles and API design best practices
Create API endpoints for CRUD operations
Implement proper HTTP status codes and response formats

modules.title: Module 7: Authentication and Authorization
modules.description: Secure your API using JWT for authentication and role-based access.
objectives: Implement user authentication with JWT
Use middleware to protect routes
Manage user roles and permissions

modules.title: Module 8: Testing and Deployment
modules.description: Test your API and deploy it to a cloud platform like Render or Railway.
objectives: Write unit and integration tests using tools like Jest or Postman
Prepare your API for production
Deploy your API to a cloud service and monitor its performance

modules.title: Module 1: Introduction to Node.js and NPM
modules.content:
## **Chapter 1: Introduction to Node.js and NPM**

Welcome to the first module of *Build REST APIs with Node.js and Express*! In this chapter, weâ€™ll lay the foundation by understanding Node.js, its runtime environment, and how to set up your development environment using Node Package Manager (NPM). By the end, youâ€™ll be ready to create your first Node.js project and manage dependencies efficiently.

---

## **What is Node.js?**

Node.js is an open-source, cross-platform **JavaScript runtime environment** that allows you to execute JavaScript code outside a web browser. Unlike traditional JavaScript, which runs in the browser, Node.js runs on the server, enabling **server-side scripting** and the development of scalable network applications.

### **Key Features of Node.js**
1. **Event-Driven & Non-Blocking I/O**: Node.js uses an event loop to handle asynchronous operations efficiently, making it ideal for I/O-heavy applications (e.g., APIs, real-time chat apps).
2. **Single-Threaded with Event Loop**: Despite being single-threaded, Node.js leverages an event-driven architecture to handle multiple requests concurrently.
3. **JavaScript Everywhere**: With Node.js, you can use JavaScript for both **frontend** and **backend** development, reducing context-switching.
4. **NPM (Node Package Manager)**: A vast ecosystem of reusable code packages (libraries/modules) that accelerates development.

### **Use Cases for Node.js**
- **RESTful APIs** (like the ones weâ€™ll build in this course)
- **Real-time applications** (e.g., chat apps, gaming servers)
- **Microservices architecture**
- **Serverless functions** (AWS Lambda, Firebase Functions)
- **Command-line tools** (e.g., Babel, Webpack)

**Example 1: Simple HTTP Server in Node.js**
Letâ€™s create a basic HTTP server to understand how Node.js handles requests:

```javascript
// server.js
const http = require('http');

const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' });
  res.end('Hello from Node.js!');
});

server.listen(3000, () => {
  console.log('Server running on http://localhost:3000');
});
```
**How it works:**
1. We import Nodeâ€™s built-in `http` module.
2. We create a server that listens on port `3000`.
3. When a request is made, the server responds with `"Hello from Node.js!"`.

Run this code using:
```bash
node server.js
```
Visit `http://localhost:3000` in your browser to see the response.

---

## **Installing Node.js and NPM**

### **Step 1: Downloading Node.js**
Node.js includes **NPM** (Node Package Manager) by default, so installing Node.js automatically installs NPM.

1. **Download Node.js** from the [official website](https://nodejs.org/).
   - Choose the **LTS (Long-Term Support)** version for stability.
   - The installer includes NPM.

2. **Run the installer** and follow the steps (default settings are fine).

3. **Verify Installation**
   Open a terminal (Command Prompt, PowerShell, or Terminal) and run:
   ```bash
   node -v
   npm -v
   ```
   You should see the installed versions, e.g.:
   ```
   v20.12.2  # Node.js version
   10.5.0    # NPM version
   ```

### **Step 2: Updating Node.js and NPM**
To update Node.js and NPM later, you can:
- **Reinstall Node.js** from the website.
- Use **NPM** to update itself:
  ```bash
  npm install -g npm@latest
  ```
- Use a version manager like **nvm** (Node Version Manager) for more control (recommended for advanced users).

**Example 2: Using NPM to Install a Package**
Letâ€™s install `express`, the web framework weâ€™ll use later:
```bash
npm install express
```
This downloads `express` and its dependencies into a `node_modules` folder.

---

## **Creating and Managing a Node.js Project with NPM**

Every Node.js project should have a `package.json` file, which acts as a **manifest** for your project. It stores metadata (name, version, dependencies) and scripts.

### **Step 1: Initialize a Node.js Project**
1. Create a new folder for your project:
   ```bash
   mkdir my-first-node-app
   cd my-first-node-app
   ```
2. Initialize the project:
   ```bash
   npm init -y
   ```
   The `-y` flag skips the interactive questionnaire (default values are used).

   This creates a `package.json` file:
   ```json
   {
     "name": "my-first-node-app",
     "version": "1.0.0",
     "description": "",
     "main": "index.js",
     "scripts": {
       "test": "echo \"Error: no test specified\" && exit 1"
     },
     "keywords": [],
     "author": "",
     "license": "ISC"
   }
   ```

### **Step 2: Adding Dependencies**
To add a package (e.g., `express`):
```bash
npm install express
```
This:
1. Downloads `express` into `node_modules`.
2. Adds `express` to `dependencies` in `package.json`:
   ```json
   "dependencies": {
     "express": "^4.18.2"
   }
   ```

### **Step 3: Running Scripts**
You can define custom scripts in `package.json` under `"scripts"`. For example:
```json
"scripts": {
  "start": "node server.js",
  "dev": "nodemon server.js"
}
```
- Run `npm start` to start your app.
- Run `npm run dev` if you have `nodemon` installed (a tool that auto-restarts the server on file changes).

### **Step 4: Managing `node_modules`**
- **Install all dependencies** listed in `package.json`:
  ```bash
  npm install
  ```
- **Remove a package**:
  ```bash
  npm uninstall express
  ```
- **Save a package as a `devDependency`** (used only in development):
  ```bash
  npm install nodemon --save-dev
  ```

---

## **Understanding the Node.js Runtime Environment**

Node.js runs on the **V8 JavaScript engine** (the same engine used in Chrome), which compiles JavaScript to native machine code for fast execution. Key components of Node.js include:

1. **Event Loop**: Handles asynchronous operations (e.g., file I/O, network requests) without blocking the main thread.
2. **Modules**: Node.js uses the `require()` function to import built-in modules (e.g., `http`, `fs`) or third-party packages.
3. **Process Object**: Provides information about the running Node.js process (e.g., `process.env` for environment variables).
4. **Buffer Class**: Handles binary data (important for file operations and network streams).

---

## **Summary**
In this chapter, we:
1. Learned what Node.js is and why itâ€™s powerful.
2. Installed Node.js and verified the installation.
3. Created a Node.js project and managed dependencies with NPM.

You now have a working Node.js environment ready for building APIs!

---

## **Key Takeaways**
- **Node.js** is a JavaScript runtime for server-side development, enabling scalable, event-driven applications.
- **NPM** is the default package manager for Node.js, allowing you to install, update, and manage libraries (packages).
- A **Node.js project** is defined by its `package.json` file, which tracks dependencies and scripts.

modules.content:
## **Chapter 8: Testing and Deployment â€“ Ensuring Reliability and Scaling Your API**

Building a REST API is only the first stepâ€”ensuring it works reliably and can scale under real-world conditions is equally critical. In this chapter, weâ€™ll focus on **testing** your API thoroughly and **deploying** it to a cloud platform like **Render** or **Railway**, so you can confidently release your application to users.

By the end of this chapter, youâ€™ll be able to:
- Write unit and integration tests using **Jest** and **Postman**
- Prepare your API for production
- Deploy your API to a cloud service
- Monitor its performance in the wild

Letâ€™s get started.

---

## **1. Why Testing Matters: Building Confidence in Your API**

Testing is not optionalâ€”itâ€™s a safety net that catches bugs before they reach production. A well-tested API:
- Ensures correctness and reliability
- Improves maintainability
- Reduces the risk of breaking changes
- Builds user trust

Weâ€™ll cover two main types of testing:
- **Unit Tests**: Test individual functions or components in isolation
- **Integration Tests**: Verify that different parts of your API work together correctly

---

## **2. Writing Unit Tests with Jest**

[Jest](https://jestjs.io/) is a popular JavaScript testing framework thatâ€™s fast, easy to set up, and works great with Express APIs. Letâ€™s walk through setting it up and writing tests.

### **Step 1: Install Jest and Related Dependencies**

```bash
npm install --save-dev jest supertest
```

- `jest`: The testing framework
- `supertest`: Lets you test HTTP servers by making requests

### **Step 2: Configure Jest**

Add a `jest.config.js` file in your project root:

```js
module.exports = {
  testEnvironment: 'node',
  clearMocks: true,
};
```

### **Step 3: Write Your First Test**

Letâ€™s test a simple route handler from a previous chapter:

```js
// app.js
const express = require('express');
const app = express();

app.get('/api/books', (req, res) => {
  res.json([{ id: 1, title: 'The Great Gatsby' }, { id: 2, title: '1984' }]);
});
```

Now, create a test file: `books.test.js`

```js
const app = require('./app');
const request = require('supertest');

describe('GET /api/books', () => {
  it('should return an array of books', async () => {
    const res = await request(app).get('/api/books');
    expect(res.statusCode).toEqual(200);
    expect(res.body).toBeInstanceOf(Array);
    expect(res.body.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
  });
});
```

### **Step 4: Run the Test**

Add a script to `package.json`:

```json
"scripts": {
  "test": "jest"
}
```

Then run:

```bash
npm test
```

âś… You should see a passing test!

---

## **3. Writing Integration Tests with Postman**

While Jest is great for backend testing, **Postman** is excellent for **API contract testing** and **integration testing** from the clientâ€™s perspective.

### **Step 1: Set Up Postman**

1. Download [Postman](https://www.postman.com/downloads/)
2. Create a new collection for your API
3. Add individual requests (GET, POST, etc.) with example payloads

### **Step 2: Test a POST Endpoint**

Letâ€™s test a `/api/books` POST endpoint that creates a new book.

**Request:**
- Method: `POST`
- URL: `http://localhost:3000/api/books`
- Headers: `Content-Type: application/json`
- Body (raw JSON):

```json
{
  "title": "The Hobbit",
  "author": "J.R.R. Tolkien"
}
```

**Tests (in Postman):**
Add this script in the "Tests" tab:

```js
pm.test("Status code is 201", function() {
  pm.response.to.have.status(201);
});

pm.test("Response has an ID", function() {
  const jsonData = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(jsonData).to.have.property('id');
});

pm.test("Title matches", function() {
  const jsonData = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(jsonData.title).to.eql("The Hobbit");
});
```

đź’ˇ **Pro Tip:** Use **Postman New Monitor** to run automated tests in the cloud on a schedule.

---

## **4. Preparing Your API for Production**

Before deploying, ensure your API is production-ready:

### âś… **Environment Variables**
Use `dotenv` to manage environment-specific settings:

```bash
npm install dotenv
```

Create `.env`:
```env
PORT=3000
MONGODB_URI=mongodb://localhost:27017/myapp
JWT_SECRET=your-secret-key
```

Update `app.js`:
```js
require('dotenv').config();
const port = process.env.PORT || 3000;
```

### âś… **Error Handling**
Add a global error handler:

```js
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(500).json({ error: 'Something went wrong!' });
});
```

### âś… **Logging**
Use `morgan` for HTTP request logging:

```bash
npm install morgan
```

```js
const morgan = require('morgan');
app.use(morgan('dev'));
```

### âś… **Security**
- Use `helmet` to set secure HTTP headers
- Use `express-rate-limit` to prevent abuse
- Sanitize user input (e.g., with `express-validator`)

```bash
npm install helmet express-rate-limit express-validator
```

```js
const helmet = require('helmet');
const rateLimit = require('express-rate-limit');

app.use(helmet());
const limiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, max: 100 });
app.use(limiter);
```

### âś… **Database Connection**
Ensure your database connection is robust:

```js
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

mongoose.connect(process.env.MONGODB_URI)
  .then(() => console.log('Connected to MongoDB'))
  .catch(err => console.error('MongoDB connection error:', err));
```

---

## **5. Deploying Your API to Render or Railway**

Now, itâ€™s time to go live! Weâ€™ll deploy to **Render** and **Railway**, two of the easiest platforms for Node.js apps.

---

### đź”ą **Deploying to Render**

**Render** offers free tier hosting with automatic HTTPS and easy environment setup.

#### **Step 1: Push Code to GitHub**
Make sure your project is in a GitHub repository.

#### **Step 2: Create a `render.yaml` (Optional)**
Render can auto-detect a Node.js app, but you can create a config file:

```yaml
services:
  - type: web
    name: node-api
    env: node
    buildCommand: npm install
    startCommand: node app.js
    envVars:
      - key: MONGODB_URI
        value: your_mongodb_uri
      - key: JWT_SECRET
        value: your_secret
```

#### **Step 3: Sign Up and Create a New Web Service**

1. Go to [Render](https://render.com)
2. Click â€śNewâ€ť > â€śWeb Serviceâ€ť
3. Connect your GitHub repo
4. Set the branch (usually `main`)
5. Configure:
   - **Name**: `node-api`
   - **Region**: Choose closest to your users
   - **Start Command**: `node app.js`
   - **Environment**: Node
6. Add environment variables from `.env` (keep secrets **hidden**â€”use Renderâ€™s secrets manager)

#### **Step 4: Deploy!**

Render will automatically build and deploy. Youâ€™ll get a live URL like:

```
https://node-api.onrender.com
```

---

### đź”ą **Deploying to Railway**

**Railway** is even simpler and great for beginners.

#### **Step 1: Sign Up**
Go to [Railway](https://railway.app) and sign up with GitHub.

#### **Step 2: Create a New Project**
1. Click â€śNew Projectâ€ť > â€śDeploy from GitHub repoâ€ť
2. Select your repo
3. Click â€śDeployâ€ť

#### **Step 3: Set Environment Variables**
Go to **Variables** in the Railway dashboard and add:

```
PORT=3000
MONGODB_URI=your_mongodb_uri
JWT_SECRET=your_secret
```

#### **Step 4: Monitor and Scale**
Railway automatically provides:
- Logs
- Metrics
- HTTP requests
- Auto-scaling

Youâ€™ll get a URL like:

```
https://node-api.up.railway.app
```

đź’ˇ **Note:** Both platforms offer free tiers with limitations. For production, consider upgrading.

---

## **6. Monitoring and Performance**

Once deployed, donâ€™t forget to monitor your API.

### âś… **Logging**
Use services like **Logflare**, **Papertrail**, or **Renderâ€™s built-in logs**.

### âś… **Uptime Monitoring**
Use **UptimeRobot** or **Pingdom** to check if your
